sentences.text: In those days, well-- I would be lying if I say Israel.
As a matter of fact, I didn't know Israel and Palestine was the same till after, after it became independent.
But the only thing we knew is Palestine in those days.
We were taught and we were, for lack of a better word, brainwashed as going to Palestine and fight for the land.
I don't remember ever being told we have to kill Arabs, or anyway-- other than just fight.
And to be honest about it, I didn't know who I was going to fight.
The only thing we knew that we had to escape the Bolsheviks, meaning and the Polish, the Russians, the Germans.
And everything is the Nazis.
And that's the only thing we had.
In the fight there, we didn't know what.
But we had to fight for homeland.
So anyhow, we joined from this camp only because they said, well, if you go, if you join the Mizrahi, Mizrahi is going to move out before this because they have better pull.
They say the Mizrahi will go out before Dror.
So, well, what's the big deal?
So we joined with the Mizrahi.
And Mizrahi keeps Shabbos, and this one don't.
I mean, it was down to the simple.
Down here, you didn't keep Shabbos, you kept the customs.
You had a Shabbat dinner and all this.
But you could ride and you could do everything on the Shabbos.
And here you had to keep Shabbos.
So you don't do it.
You know, it was no big deal.
Because at that time religion per SE didn't mean to us because all during the, the, the, they war and all during this stuff, we sort of benched.
And going back, I remember my zayde, who was a big religious guy.
And when my uncles got sick, and we were just attacked, and we didn't have any food, he just took a knife and cut his beard off and unbuttoned his halat.
He says, there is no God in heaven that would just let you, let us and the kids suffer so much.
And that stuck in our mind very much.
So we knew about, you know, the customs to some degree, and they were teaching us.
But it didn't matter if it was in a, in a Orthodox or whatever going to Palestine.
So we jumped around till we came to, to this one camp, and that was near Rosenheim.
And we had-- we were actually trained in 1947-- we were back at the kibbutz Dror.
And we had a, a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was our [NON-ENGLISH] leader.
And we joined that group.
And then we knew we're going to go on this and this ship.
We were trained that we were going to go to Palestine.
We'll have to go to France.
And this was already in '46, early '47.
We had about six, eight months training.
We knew-- we didn't know the name of the ship at that time.
But we knew that there was-- we knew there's going to be a big crowd.
And we trained for-- each kid had their own mission.
In 194-- and actually in 1946, we were told that we will not be able to speak.
We will not be able to take any pictures.
We will be going as-- under a Colombian flag.
We knew our destiny.
We knew we were going to leave in '47.
I've always said that this is part of the-- it was a big, massive plan.
It was planned.
And the only thing we had to wait for that they didn't know is what kind of ship we're going to have and where it comes from.
But I learned that later that this ship came from America.
It was actually a-- one of those-- like a cruise ship type of a ship.
And it was originally was supposed to hold 500 people.
And we were 4,500 kids on that boat.
They tore everything out.
We were laying four high, stacked, like in bunks.
And anyhow, we had to go.
We wound up in France.
And we laid there for about, I think it was maybe two, three months waiting till everybody met.
And I remember when we left Germany on the trucks, it was big lines.
And they met from, from different DP camps.
And we all met in France.
And it was trucks after trucks.
And we were set in an outside field like in a bivouac.
And we had those cot beds, military cot beds.
And they had tents.
And it was, it was, it was May, June.
It was still sort of the days was burning hot, and the nights was freezing cold.
And we lay down there till everybody met.
And then they took us on the flag-- on the ship.
And we flew under Colombian flag.
I think it was in Port-de-Bouc, France.
And we went on the boat.
And we were told-- and each one was in their own groups.
And we had kommandos set up.
And when we got on the ship, each one was assigned to different positions.
We knew-- our mission at that time was to get into and fight for to be put on to Cyprus.
Anything else is not acceptable.
So if it means we get killed, our mission was to get to Cyprus.
And we had to fight not to let them take us in here.
But we knew we would probably will get caught.
We knew we'd get to Cyprus.
And once you're in Cyprus, they figure it takes you six months, a year, you'll get into Palestine.
I mean, this is what we were trained for and this is what we was told.
So when we left France, actually the English ships followed us.
And it took us about seven days or so.
And we were only one wooden vessel.
It took seven military warships to catch us.
And what they did is they, they hit us one from this side, and another one caught us and hit us back, and just kept going till we got water.
But we fought with them for 7 and 1/2 hours.
My and my sister's job was we were right on top of the deck, and we had to grab the hand grenades when they threw in the tear gas.
We had to grab those and throw them back overboard.
And we had the big knives.
And when they threw those, those ropes over, down there, we had to cut the ropes off.
That was our job, staying in the-- you know, at the top.
You know how the ship goes, and then the others?
That's where Fay and I was.
And we did a pretty good job.
We're crying.
They used to give us wet towels to put on your eyes, so when the tear gas hits you put it on.
And then once the burning gets over, you went back on.
And we fought them for seven, 7 and 1/2 hours.
And we got into Haifa.
And they unloaded us when we, when we took water.
They put us on four ships on the way back.
And they, they took our boat.
First they tried to go back to France.
And then France we refused to go out.
And we went on strikes.
But we just refused to get out in France.
No.

The only time we knew is-- when we got transferred, when we got transferred from Haifa, and we were put on the boat-- in history sometimes, the people who make history don't know they're making history.
And in retrospect, when I think, we were put down in the hull of a cargo ship.
That's where we are.
And then the British were walking on top of the mesh wires.
And the soldiers, they were mean to us.
They were mean.
And our mission, once we found out-- we had-- when they transferred it-- and this was told to me later, but I know for a fact, we had two Haganist in our boat laying with us.
And matter of fact, we had those-- just got back-- there was one guy, this British soldier, who was walking on top of the deck, and all this dirt and all this used to fall down on us.
And must have been about 500, 600 of us people, kids were laying, just lay on the floor with blankets and that's it.
And they used to through down crackers to eat.
They bringed us in crackers and water.
And that's all we had.
And this Haganah, he just climbed up a rope and grabbed him, and just pulled him right under, just stabbed him to death.
And, and we were constantly told that we will not get off in France.
Well, France was a-- and this was told to us, that France would not force us on, or the English couldn't because this was a French zone.
And the English didn't have any authority to do it unless they got the French permission.
And we was told that France will not give them permission to force us off because it was their land.
And it was their occupied thing.
It was not a British zone.
So they-- then we came over down there.
And think we stayed there for about three or four days.
And they tried to get us off down there.
But we just kept on refusing.
And we were told we're not getting off.
You can't.
There was a few people that went off.
There was about three or four kids got off in Israel, too, in Palestine at that time.
They were sick.
But our duty was not.
Our duty was to get into Cyprus.
So they were not allowed to force us off in, in, in France.
So from there they took us back into Hamburg.
Hamburg was a British zone port, seaport.
So then there, they flooded us out.
And they just put in hoses of water and just flooded us out.
So we got off down there.
And it took us a long time.
And I still remember seeing the stories.
And we had rabble-rousers.
We don't do it.
We're going to get in.
And at that time, we knew that Israel is going to become a state.
That was after we got off.
So we became-- what do you call it?-- more stubborn.
The more they did, and the more stubborn, the more the Haganahs-- we, we went through this.
And they, they had some bright-- again, in retrospect, they had some bright young people trained.
And they knew how to get the blood boiling of all us young kids.
And I mean, we would, we would kill anybody, just all he had to do is tell us.
And that's how wound up we were.
Anyhow, when we got to Hamburg, we were in a DP camp held by the British, actually barbed wired with soldiers.
And we used to get powdered eggs and crackers.
And the Red Cross used to come, but no Jewish organizations, to the best of my knowledge.
And this was already in '47.
And '47, we stayed down there as a prisoner.
Now, Germany offered citizenship to anybody who wanted to go out.
I still have a lot of permission to go-- anybody who want to leave the camps could leave the camps.
But anyway, if you stayed, you were an actual prisoner.
In order to leave the camp, you had to sign a-- you got a paper, and you had to sign a paper that you're going out and you're going to settle somewhere in Germany.
And our mission was not to give in.
We have to go back to Palestine.
So kibbutz Dror was stubborn, whatever that was.
So anyhow, we, we stayed there for, for quite a while.
And they had the machine guns every 10, 15 feet, wire meshes.
And they had those British soldiers with the skirts-- kilts, and all that.
And I remember as a kid that we used to joke about them.
But we were actually in a prison camp.
And in 1948 we were visited by my Uncle Sam.
He was coming to America.
And he told us that Mom and my family, everybody is already in America.
And my mother wants us to come here.
He was in Austria.
And he was ready to come to America to, his visas.
So he came over.
And, like I said, it was sort of a tug-of-war between my sister and I.
We went through so much.
And then, my sister and I-- my uncle came in.
He gave us regards from Mom.
And he says it's all there.
They can make the arrangements.
They'll take us if we want to go instead of going to Palestine.
He come over.
Mom wants us.
And, you know, he gave us the whole thing.
And we said, we'll let you know.
And we didn't want to go.
But after a while, we laid in those camps, and we didn't have the slightest idea as to when we're going to go.
And this was already middle of '48.
And I don't know any dates or nothing.
And my sister and I looked at each other, and said, Irv, we were fighting enough.
Let's go.
And by that time, we used to get some letters from Mom.
My uncle already got to America.
And letters from America, and the films, and the things they were saying, it was, you know, everything.
The streets are paved with honey and gold.
Money grows on trees.
And, you know, and the cowboys and Indians.
I mean, this is what you were trained.
And you live a good life.
And there's no fighting.
You don't have to hide.
You don't have to be scared.
You don't have to carry a rifle.
You can-- it's free.
So my sister and I looked and we said, that's enough.
We were just tired of fighting.
We were just tired of, of not being able to, to relax.
And she says, why don't we finally give in and, and join the rest of the family, go there and just become ourselves.
By that time-- till we die.
Because we already lived a lifetime.
And I know I probably passed up a lot of this stuff.
So we decided that we just don't want to.
But we had a bad dilemma.

When you're in a group and tight as we are, and when you say that you're going to leave them to go, not to go to Palestine, you become sort of an enemy of the group, that you're a traitor to the group that you were.
And this was a lot of responsibility.
So when we finally decided what we'd do, which was took a lot of our inner soul searching and all this.
But Fay and I, we were just tired.
We were tired of fighting.
We were tired of running.
We were tire of-- we just wanted to live a little bit.
And we just wanted to know that I can have something to eat whenever I wanted instead of staying in line, instead of asking and begging for it.
Because most of our life was oriented.
You ate from such and such time.
You didn't have the freedoms.
You didn't have anything.
And we were just tired.
You were just on our own.
It's hard to explain a feeling that a 12 and a 14-year-old have at that age.
But it was just like-- a lot of times I hear that from my mom talking.
And she's 85.
She's just, I'm tired of life, you know?
This is what we had.
So we decided that we're going to go to America, join our friends.
So we went up with our friends in the camp, becoming traitors, if you may.
Because we left them to go this.
And we didn't believe in going to fight our cause.
Because we still had a cause.
We still had to go over and take Palestine.
And we decided to leave it.
So we got it.
We went in, and we told the commandant that we want to go.
It took us two or three days till we finally got arranged for a telephone call to America to see through the camp.
And we told them we want to go.
And they had to start making papers to bring us out.
So when we announced that, we had to get on the quota to come to America.
Although my mom and them we're going to take us out.
But a quota-- And we couldn't stay in this camp.
And it's-- you got to, you got to realize what the times and situation is.
There's two kids who are without a country, without a camp, and without a purpose, and traitors.
So when you went from one camp, a DP camp, where you going?
You're going to America.
It was sort of a dirty word to us.
So we had to wait for a quota.
Quota system in those days were-- so you had to have the right time.
We were a sister and brother, so you had to wait.
The time, sister and brother, the age, everything had to be in order to be your turn.
And we waited from '48 till-- we waited over a year till we got the quota to come to America.
And the only reason we come is we changed our age in order to, to meet that.
Meanwhile, we, we were-- I don't even know all the camps.
We were in DP camps.
We were assigned to DP camps because we were waiting to go to America.
And most of DP camps in those days were geared to go to Israel.
And it was, it was, it was a hellish type of a life till we came to, to Bremen.
From Bremen we were there for about four to six months.
We stayed down there.
And this was already in the, in the French zone.
We were.
It's not actually a DP camp.
It was a-- it was not a camp.
It was sort of a house where your waited.
Yeah, it was.
And we were sustained by it.
We were too young.
We didn't live, pay rent, or whatever.
We were in a, in an institutional type of--
It was supported by, I think by HIAS, or Red Cross, or it was a DP organ, which was supported in those days by-- DP camps were supported by the countries.
And orphans-- we were still considered an orphan.
We were still considered as displaced.
But we were there, to be honest, I don't know who all the supporters were.
But we were fed three times a day.
We had clothes.
And the life down in the DP camps was much like a socialized-- then in today you're-- especially in the kibbutzim was socialized.
Today you wore those shoes, next week somebody else wore the socks.
When you turn them to wash, went in the group.
You went-- you know, you lived in this barracks.
You went and you had 10 pair of socks that fit you fine.
If not, you didn't have any.
This was type of a life DP camps was.
But we stayed there till our quota came in.
And of course, you were freer.
You could go wherever you wanted to.
You didn't have to ask for permission to go into the city or ask for permission.
And when our quota came, we finally boarded.
And we went to New York.
We landed on April the 6th of 1949.
And the, the HIAS met us there on the ship.
And they picked us up and held us overnight.
And then they-- early in the morning, they put us on a train.
Or is it that evening they put us on the train?
Because the 7th I was here.
So they kept us-- early in the morning, we got off of the ship.
They kept us there.
We ate dinner.
They put us on a train.
We rode all night long.
And on April the 7th, I think it was about 9 o'clock in the morning, we came in to Columbus, Ohio.
Yeah, we had to be.
We had to be in order to-- in order to go, in order to go to Palestine, you had to be an orphan to go and be there.
Because if you had parents, you could have, then your parents would have had to be with you.
The camps that-- the groups that we joined were strictly for orphans.
But they had different DP camps.
And they had different groups where parents and their kids could go and go to Israel.
But is not-- my sister and I wanted to be in a fighting, a mission-oriented.
And this was part of it.
I would say, in retrospect, it was because I was brainwashed by the Haganahs.
And they were very good leaders.
And we'd seen so much fighting for all this that we know that we didn't want to through that.
And we wanted to be in Palestine on the land of what we wanted to be.
Because anywhere else, we knew we're going to be in programs.
We're going to be persecuted again.
So that was our mission at that time.
We always felt that you got survived for what?
And that was our purpose is to go there.
But we finally gave up.
And we just-- too much of the patriotism, and it was just too much for us.
So yeah, we could have come.
A lot of families did.
But we wanted to get there faster.
It's-- but that's enough.
We want to get out.
And my mother always felt that together we're going to get killed, separated we'd be alive.
Even to this day, when we good to-- when I go to shows or wherever, I try to sit in ends.
Too many Jews, I'm scared.
My mother don't like to go to the theaters or other places where there's a lot.
Now, since and she's older, I don't think she thinks of it.
But we never went where there's big Jewish crowds.

And when I used to be active in, which I am in the Yom Ashura and others, one of the first thing, or Yom Kippur or Rosh Hashana, one of the first things when I was in the boards was to make sure we have security police always, no matter what I was active in because of that fear.
Although, I'm not such a Zionist, I don't think I am a Zionist anymore as I used to be.
But I still feel I'm [NON-ENGLISH].
And as long as a I'll be in a way down there, I still think a goy is a goy.
And America or whatever, you're still a Jew, and your number is only, who knows in whose hands.
Life in America was very good.
First experiences, and I shared it quite often, is my brother was already here.
He spoke good English.
And here I am, 13 years old, I'm being put in a first grade.
The chairs were too small, and the tables were too small.
So they had to find a place for me to sit, not necessarily to study, not according to my knowledge, where it is.
So third grade chairs fit me a lot better, so they put me in the third grade.
And my brother spoke English.
And he used to tutor me a lot.
And I started off at Ohio Avenue School.
And I was very fortunate.
I met a teacher and a principal at that time who spoke German.
And I spoke fluently German, and Polish, and Ukraine, and Jewish-- Yiddish-- and no English.
So I used to stay after school, and she took special interest on me.
And we used to tell her some of the life stories.
And she sort of got a liking to me.
And so I went in there during the day, and I went at night school, and I had her teaching.
Then I started work in the Central Market, started working for Martins, and worked part-time for Haps.
Helped my dad, he worked for Franklin Auto Parts, [INAUDIBLE], in this junk yard, and worked for [INAUDIBLE].
But most of the time is my brother down here, he already spoke good English.
So he used to take me around.
And one incident-- if you can picture the contrast between America and Europe.
In Europe, after the war, it's not that I was afraid of policemen, but we were always afraid of uniforms.
Because those uniforms we knew is danger to you, especially if you are Jewish.
And in Europe before the war, even during the war, you were afraid because I knew that I was circumcised.
And that fear in Europe was always hanging over.
So they'd check.
Is it circumcised?
You can get killed.
Because this was the times of it.
And we constantly taught, make sure you don't caught.
Because if you get caught, you're going to be caught.
And you got to go and check you.
You're a Jew.
You're going to be killed.
So when we came over here, and I was in the Central Market, we used to have this one policeman.
And I shared that story with a lot of them the first time that I felt freedom.
Freedom is hard to express, a feeling.
And the example is I use, and that happened to me, is my brother says, Irv-- and I used to be scared.
I seen a policeman, I used to hide.
And he says, Irv, you're in America.
Down here you can even hit a policeman, he won't do anything to you.
[LAUGHTER] And this is a fact.
So we were in the Central Market.
We used to have-- this one policeman used to walk the beat.
I was staying on a Saturday.
And this happened in 1949, the summer of 1949.
And I went over to him, and I hit him.
And I ran over to the stand.
He stood down there, dumbfounded.
And I went over again, and I hit him.
He didn't chase me.
He didn't do anything.
The expressions when I did this-- And then my brother told-- I didn't speak English.
I didn't know what it is.
And I said-- and he went over and he told him.
And we became good friends afterwards.
And I mean, this guy, this guy must have been at least six feet, especially when you're little.
And I found out from that moment on what freedom is all about.
And I wouldn't trade it.
I still don't feel quote, '100% safe.' I felt that I could tell the world that I was Jewish.
I never hid it, the point that I was Jewish.
And a matter of fact, I used to brag about it in the schools or this.
And the way I did that is-- and I still do that-- is for habit, and it's with my name, S-Z-A-M-E-S.
And almost any place you go, what kind of name is that?
It's unusual.
And I say, it's Polish spelling, it's Jewish, Polish spelling, Szames.
And I was said Polish Jewish, or Jewish Polish spelling.
I've always used that since I came to America.
And I still do it up to this day, whenever I go somewhere.
I think I told you that.
I said it was a Jewish but Polish spelling, Szames, but in Polish, spelled Polish.
This is what America is.
I don't think it's the most freedomest.
But for me, it was a land of opportunity.
Not that anybody gave me, but it gave me the freedom, a lot of freedom that I could do.
I could work hard and make, and nobody took it away from me.
I wasn't scared.
I wasn't scared somebody is going take it-- robbing, but take it.
